question:
3. In a round-robin tournament with \( n \) players (each pair of players plays one game) in which there are no draws, the numbers of wins scored by the players are \( s_{1}, s_{2}, \ldots, s_{n} \). Prove that a necessary and sufficient condition for the existence of 3 players, \( A, B, C \), such that \( A \) beat \( B, B \) beat \( C \), and \( C \) beat \( A \) is
\[
s_{1}^{2}+s_{2}^{2}+\cdots+s_{n}^{2}<(n-1)(n)(2 n-1)^{\prime} 6
\]
solution:
Solution. For any tournament outcome \( T \), let
\[
U(T)=s_{1}{ }^{2}+s_{2}{ }^{2}+\cdots+s_{n}{ }^{2}
\]

The outcome of a round-robin tournament will be called transitive if and only if there are no examples of three players \( A, B \), and \( C \) such that \( A \) beat \( B, B \) beat \( C \), and \( C \) beat \( A \). In this case, "beat" is a transitive linear-order relation on the set of players, so we can number the players \( P_{1}, P_{2}, \ldots, P_{n} \) so that \( P_{i} \) beat \( P_{j} \) if and only if \( i>j \). Then the final scores of the players are, respectively,
\[
0,1, \ldots, n-1
\]

Hence in the transitive case we have
\[
U(T)=0^{2}+1^{2}+\cdots+(n-1)^{2}=(n-1) n(2 n-1) / 6
\]

This proves that the given condition is sufficient.
Now consider a non-transitive tournament outcome, that is, suppose there are three players, \( A, B \). and \( C \), such that \( A \) beat \( B, B \) beat \( C \), and \( C \) beat \( A \). We may assume that \( s_{A} \) is the least of the numbers \( s_{A}, s_{B} \), and \( s_{C} \); then \( s_{A} \leq s_{B} \). Now reverse the outcome of the match between \( A \) and \( B \). We get a new tournament outcome in which \( A \) 's score is \( s_{A}-1 \), \( B \) s score is \( s_{B}+1 \), and every other player's score remains the same. Then \( U \) is increased by
\[
\left(s_{A}-1\right)^{2}-s_{A}^{2}+\left(s_{B}+1\right)^{2}-s_{B}^{2}=2\left(s_{B}-s_{A}\right)+2>0 .
\]

Thus any non-transitive tournament outcome can be changed so as to increase \( U \). But it is impossible to do this indefinitely because the number of possible outcomes is finite. So starting with a non-transitive outcome, after a finite number of such changes the outcome becomes transitive and \( U \) increases to \( (n-1) n(2 n-1) / 6 \). Hence for a non-transitive outcome
\[
U(T)<(n-1) n(2 n-1) / 6
\]

Thus the given condition is necessary.
Remark. Tournaments have been extensively analyzed. See J. W. Moon, Topics on Tournaments. Holt, Rinehart and Winston, New York, 1969.

descriptive_long.question:
3. In a round-robin tournament with \( playerscount \) players (each pair of players plays one game) in which there are no draws, the numbers of wins scored by the players are \( scoreone, scoretwo, \ldots, scoren \). Prove that a necessary and sufficient condition for the existence of 3 players, \( playera, playerb, playerc \), such that \( playera \) beat \( playerb, playerb \) beat \( playerc \), and \( playerc \) beat \( playera \) is
\[
scoreone^{2}+scoretwo^{2}+\cdots+scoren^{2}<(playerscount-1)(playerscount)(2 playerscount-1)^{\prime} 6
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. For any tournament outcome \( tourneyoutcome \), let
\[
scoretotal(tourneyoutcome)=scoreone^{2}+scoretwo^{2}+\cdots+scoren^{2}
\]

The outcome of a round-robin tournament will be called transitive if and only if there are no examples of three players \( playera, playerb \), and \( playerc \) such that \( playera \) beat \( playerb, playerb \) beat \( playerc \), and \( playerc \) beat \( playera \). In this case, "beat" is a transitive linear-order relation on the set of players, so we can number the players \( playerone, P_{2}, \ldots, playern \) so that \( playeri \) beat \( playerj \) if and only if \( indexi>indexj \). Then the final scores of the players are, respectively,
\[
0,1, \ldots, playerscount-1
\]

Hence in the transitive case we have
\[
scoretotal(tourneyoutcome)=0^{2}+1^{2}+\cdots+(playerscount-1)^{2}=(playerscount-1) playerscount(2 playerscount-1) / 6
\]

This proves that the given condition is sufficient.
Now consider a non-transitive tournament outcome, that is, suppose there are three players, \( playera, playerb \). and \( playerc \), such that \( playera \) beat \( playerb, playerb \) beat \( playerc \), and \( playerc \) beat \( playera \). We may assume that \( scorea \) is the least of the numbers \( scorea, scoreb \), and \( scorec \); then \( scorea \leq scoreb \). Now reverse the outcome of the match between \( playera \) and \( playerb \). We get a new tournament outcome in which \( playera \)'s score is \( scorea-1 \), \( playerb \)'s score is \( scoreb+1 \), and every other player's score remains the same. Then \( scoretotal \) is increased by
\[
\left(scorea-1\right)^{2}-scorea^{2}+\left(scoreb+1\right)^{2}-scoreb^{2}=2\left(scoreb-scorea\right)+2>0 .
\]

Thus any non-transitive tournament outcome can be changed so as to increase \( scoretotal \). But it is impossible to do this indefinitely because the number of possible outcomes is finite. So starting with a non-transitive outcome, after a finite number of such changes the outcome becomes transitive and \( scoretotal \) increases to \( (playerscount-1) playerscount(2 playerscount-1) / 6 \). Hence for a non-transitive outcome
\[
scoretotal(tourneyoutcome)<(playerscount-1) playerscount(2 playerscount-1) / 6
\]

Thus the given condition is necessary.
Remark. Tournaments have been extensively analyzed. See J. W. Moon, Topics on Tournaments. Holt, Rinehart and Winston, New York, 1969.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
3. In a round-robin tournament with \( keystroke \) players (each pair of players plays one game) in which there are no draws, the numbers of wins scored by the players are \( orchardia, pineconed, \ldots, riverbank \). Prove that a necessary and sufficient condition for the existence of 3 players, \( partridge, woodpeck, kingfisher \), such that \( partridge \) beat \( woodpeck, woodpeck \) beat \( kingfisher \), and \( kingfisher \) beat \( partridge \) is
\[
orchardia^{2}+pineconed^{2}+\cdots+riverbank^{2}<(keystroke-1)(keystroke)(2 keystroke-1)^{\prime} 6
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. For any tournament outcome \( horizongo \), let
\[
flagstone(horizongo)=orchardia { }^{2}+pineconed { }^{2}+\cdots+riverbank { }^{2}
\]

The outcome of a round-robin tournament will be called transitive if and only if there are no examples of three players \( partridge, woodpeck \), and \( kingfisher \) such that \( partridge \) beat \( woodpeck, woodpeck \) beat \( kingfisher \), and \( kingfisher \) beat \( partridge \). In this case, "beat" is a transitive linear-order relation on the set of players, so we can number the players \( lanternone, P_{2}, \ldots, lanternmax \) so that \( lanternrow \) beat \( lanterncol \) if and only if \( quiltwork>snowflake \). Then the final scores of the players are, respectively,
\[
0,1, \ldots, keystroke-1
\]

Hence in the transitive case we have
\[
flagstone(horizongo)=0^{2}+1^{2}+\cdots+(keystroke-1)^{2}=(keystroke-1) keystroke(2 keystroke-1) / 6
\]

This proves that the given condition is sufficient.
Now consider a non-transitive tournament outcome, that is, suppose there are three players, \( partridge, woodpeck \). and \( kingfisher \), such that \( partridge \) beat \( woodpeck, woodpeck \) beat \( kingfisher \), and \( kingfisher \) beat \( partridge \). We may assume that \( moonstone \) is the least of the numbers \( moonstone, sandstone \), and \( blackberry \); then \( moonstone \leq sandstone \). Now reverse the outcome of the match between \( partridge \) and \( woodpeck \). We get a new tournament outcome in which \( partridge \) 's score is \( moonstone-1 \), \( woodpeck \) s score is \( sandstone+1 \), and every other player's score remains the same. Then \( flagstone \) is increased by
\[
\left(moonstone-1\right)^{2}-moonstone^{2}+\left(sandstone+1\right)^{2}-sandstone^{2}=2\left(sandstone-moonstone\right)+2>0 .
\]

Thus any non-transitive tournament outcome can be changed so as to increase \( flagstone \). But it is impossible to do this indefinitely because the number of possible outcomes is finite. So starting with a non-transitive outcome, after a finite number of such changes the outcome becomes transitive and \( flagstone \) increases to \( (keystroke-1) keystroke(2 keystroke-1) / 6 \). Hence for a non-transitive outcome
\[
flagstone(horizongo)<(keystroke-1) keystroke(2 keystroke-1) / 6
\]

Thus the given condition is necessary.
Remark. Tournaments have been extensively analyzed. See J. W. Moon, Topics on Tournaments. Holt, Rinehart and Winston, New York, 1969.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
3. In a round-robin tournament with \( emptiness \) players (each pair of players plays one game) in which there are no draws, the numbers of wins scored by the players are \( losscorea, losscoreb, \ldots, losscorez \). Prove that a necessary and sufficient condition for the existence of 3 players, \( observera, observerb, observerc \), such that \( observera \) beat \( observerb, observerb \) beat \( observerc \), and \( observerc \) beat \( observera \) is
\[
losscorea^{2}+losscoreb^{2}+\cdots+losscorez^{2}<(emptiness-1)(emptiness)(2 emptiness-1)^{\prime} 6
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. For any tournament outcome \( inception \), let
\[
diminish(inception)=losscorea{ }^{2}+losscoreb{ }^{2}+\cdots+losscorez{ }^{2}
\]

The outcome of a round-robin tournament will be called transitive if and only if there are no examples of three players \( observera, observerb \), and \( observerc \) such that \( observera \) beat \( observerb, observerb \) beat \( observerc \), and \( observerc \) beat \( observera \). In this case, "beat" is a transitive linear-order relation on the set of players, so we can number the players \( spectatora, P_{2}, \ldots, spectatorz \) so that \( spectatori \) beat \( spectatorj \) if and only if \( constantone>constanttwo \). Then the final scores of the players are, respectively,
\[
0,1, \ldots, emptiness-1
\]

Hence in the transitive case we have
\[
diminish(inception)=0^{2}+1^{2}+\cdots+(emptiness-1)^{2}=(emptiness-1) emptiness(2 emptiness-1) / 6
\]

This proves that the given condition is sufficient.
Now consider a non-transitive tournament outcome, that is, suppose there are three players, \( observera, observerb \). and \( observerc \), such that \( observera \) beat \( observerb, observerb \) beat \( observerc \), and \( observerc \) beat \( observera \). We may assume that \( losscorep \) is the least of the numbers \( losscorep, losscoreq \), and \( losscorer \); then \( losscorep \leq losscoreq \). Now reverse the outcome of the match between \( observera \) and \( observerb \). We get a new tournament outcome in which \( observera \) 's score is \( losscorep-1 \), \( observerb \) s score is \( losscoreq+1 \), and every other player's score remains the same. Then \( diminish \) is increased by
\[
\left(losscorep-1\right)^{2}-losscorep^{2}+\left(losscoreq+1\right)^{2}-losscoreq^{2}=2\left(losscoreq-losscorep\right)+2>0 .
\]

Thus any non-transitive tournament outcome can be changed so as to increase \( diminish \). But it is impossible to do this indefinitely because the number of possible outcomes is finite. So starting with a non-transitive outcome, after a finite number of such changes the outcome becomes transitive and \( diminish \) increases to \( (emptiness-1) emptiness(2 emptiness-1) / 6 \). Hence for a non-transitive outcome
\[
diminish(inception)<(emptiness-1) emptiness(2 emptiness-1) / 6
\]

Thus the given condition is necessary.
Remark. Tournaments have been extensively analyzed. See J. W. Moon, Topics on Tournaments. Holt, Rinehart and Winston, New York, 1969.

garbled_string.question:
3. In a round-robin tournament with \( byoetfwa \) players (each pair of players plays one game) in which there are no draws, the numbers of wins scored by the players are \( qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, \ldots, vmbdploe \). Prove that a necessary and sufficient condition for the existence of 3 players, \( glovwzrb, rkhdwqsp, zmlqkyev \), such that \( glovwzrb \) beat \( rkhdwqsp, rkhdwqsp \) beat \( zmlqkyev \), and \( zmlqkyev \) beat \( glovwzrb \) is
\[
qzxwvtnp^{2}+hjgrksla^{2}+\cdots+vmbdploe^{2}<(byoetfwa-1)(byoetfwa)(2 byoetfwa-1)^{\prime} 6
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. For any tournament outcome \( ghsapzqm \), let
\[
dvrowysp(ghsapzqm)=qzxwvtnp{ }^{2}+hjgrksla{ }^{2}+\cdots+vmbdploe{ }^{2}
\]

The outcome of a round-robin tournament will be called transitive if and only if there are no examples of three players \( glovwzrb, rkhdwqsp \), and \( zmlqkyev \) such that \( glovwzrb \) beat \( rkhdwqsp, rkhdwqsp \) beat \( zmlqkyev \), and \( zmlqkyev \) beat \( glovwzrb \). In this case, "beat" is a transitive linear-order relation on the set of players, so we can number the players \( uixlbtga, P_{2}, \ldots, pzkvyrof \) so that \( yclhmdse \) beat \( nmkatlhe \) if and only if \( pfmsuqve>kzwtrlha \). Then the final scores of the players are, respectively,
\[
0,1, \ldots, byoetfwa-1
\]

Hence in the transitive case we have
\[
dvrowysp(ghsapzqm)=0^{2}+1^{2}+\cdots+(byoetfwa-1)^{2}=(byoetfwa-1) byoetfwa(2 byoetfwa-1) / 6
\]

This proves that the given condition is sufficient.
Now consider a non-transitive tournament outcome, that is, suppose there are three players, \( glovwzrb, rkhdwqsp \). and \( zmlqkyev \), such that \( glovwzrb \) beat \( rkhdwqsp, rkhdwqsp \) beat \( zmlqkyev \), and \( zmlqkyev \) beat \( glovwzrb \). We may assume that \( rtfyqksw \) is the least of the numbers \( rtfyqksw, xobnmdqh \), and \( wselkzra \); then \( rtfyqksw \leq xobnmdqh \). Now reverse the outcome of the match between \( glovwzrb \) and \( rkhdwqsp \). We get a new tournament outcome in which \( glovwzrb \) 's score is \( rtfyqksw-1 \), \( rkhdwqsp \)'s score is \( xobnmdqh+1 \), and every other player's score remains the same. Then \( dvrowysp \) is increased by
\[
\left(rtfyqksw-1\right)^{2}-rtfyqksw^{2}+\left(xobnmdqh+1\right)^{2}-xobnmdqh^{2}=2\left(xobnmdqh-rtfyqksw\right)+2>0 .
\]

Thus any non-transitive tournament outcome can be changed so as to increase \( dvrowysp \). But it is impossible to do this indefinitely because the number of possible outcomes is finite. So starting with a non-transitive outcome, after a finite number of such changes the outcome becomes transitive and \( dvrowysp \) increases to \( (byoetfwa-1) byoetfwa(2 byoetfwa-1) / 6 \). Hence for a non-transitive outcome
\[
dvrowysp(ghsapzqm)<(byoetfwa-1) byoetfwa(2 byoetfwa-1) / 6
\]

Thus the given condition is necessary.
Remark. Tournaments have been extensively analyzed. See J. W. Moon, Topics on Tournaments. Holt, Rinehart and Winston, New York, 1969.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $n\ge 3$ be an integer.  
For every integer $r\ge 0$ define the falling factorial  

\[
(x)_{r}=x(x-1)\cdots (x-r+1),\qquad 
\text{with the conventions }(x)_{0}=1\text{ and }(x)_{r}=0\text{ whenever }x<r.
\]

For a round-robin tournament $T$ on $n$ players (each pair of players meets once, no draws) denote by  

\[
s_{1},\ldots ,s_{n}
\]

the numbers of wins of the players and put  

\[
U_{r}(T)=\sum_{i=1}^{n}(s_{i})_{r}.
\]

Further introduce the constants  

\[
M_{r}(n)=\frac{(n)_{\,r+1}}{r+1}= \frac{n(n-1)\cdots (n-r)}{r+1}.
\]

1.  Show that for $r=2$ the following equivalence holds:

\[
\boxed{\;T\text{ contains a directed $3$-cycle}\;\Longleftrightarrow\;
U_{2}(T)<M_{2}(n)=\dfrac{n(n-1)(n-2)}{3}\;}. \tag{$\star$}
\]

2.  Prove that $r=2$ is the only non-negative integer $r$ for which an equivalence of the form $(\star)$ is valid for every $n\ge 3$.  More precisely, establish

(a)  $U_{0}(T)$ and $U_{1}(T)$ are constant (hence they cannot detect $3$-cycles);

(b)  for every $r\ge 3$ there exists an integer $n\ge r+1$ and a tournament $T$ on $n$ vertices that contains a directed $3$-cycle yet satisfies $U_{r}(T)=M_{r}(n)$, so that $(\star)$ fails.

Consequently $U_{2}$ is the unique member of the family $U_{r}$ that detects $3$-cycles in tournaments.



--------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
Part 1 - The quadratic case $r=2$

Step 1. Landau's theorem and a majorisation statement.  
Arrange the score sequence in non-decreasing order

\[
t_{1}\le t_{2}\le\cdots\le t_{n}.
\]

Landau's theorem asserts that for every $m=1,\ldots ,n$

\[
\sum_{i\le m}t_{i}\;\ge\; \frac{m(m-1)}{2},\tag{1.1}
\]

with equality for all $m$ simultaneously if and only if the tournament is transitive (acyclic).

Introduce the reference vector  

\[
a=(0,1,\ldots ,n-1).\tag{1.2}
\]

Because $\sum_{i\le m}a_{i}=m(m-1)/2$, inequality (1.1) rewrites as  

\[
\forall m:\;\sum_{i\le m}t_{i}\ge\sum_{i\le m}a_{i},
\qquad\text{with equality for }m=n. \tag{1.3}
\]

Thus, under the \emph{ascending} convention for majorisation,

\[
t\;\text{majorises}\;a,\qquad\text{briefly }t\succ a.\tag{1.4}
\]

Step 2. Schur-concavity of the quadratic falling factorial.  
Let $g(x)=x(x-1)$.  On $\mathbf Z$ we have  

\[
\Delta^{2}g(x)=g(x+2)-2g(x+1)+g(x)=2>0,
\]

so $g$ is convex.  A classical result (Hardy-Littlewood-Polya, inequality theory) says that \emph{for vectors sorted in the same order} a convex symmetric sum is \emph{Schur-concave} under the \emph{ascending} convention:  

\[
u\succ v,\;u\ne v\;\Longrightarrow\;\Phi(u)<\Phi(v), \tag{1.5}
\]

where  

\[
\Phi(z_{1},\ldots ,z_{n})=\sum_{i=1}^{n}g(z_{i})
=\sum_{i=1}^{n}(z_{i})_{2}=U_{2}(T).
\]

(The direction of the inequality is reversed compared with the more usual \emph{descending} convention; that is the only adaptation one must keep in mind.)

Step 3. Extremality of the transitive tournament.  
Apply (1.5) to $u=t$, $v=a$.  When $T$ is not transitive we have $t\ne a$ and thus  

\[
U_{2}(T)=\Phi(t)<\Phi(a). \tag{1.6}
\]

If $T$ \emph{is} transitive then $t=a$, hence $U_{2}(T)=\Phi(a)$.

Step 4. Evaluation of $\Phi(a)$.  
For the transitive scores $0,1,\ldots ,n-1$,

\[
\Phi(a)=\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}k(k-1)
=\frac{n(n-1)(n-2)}{3}=M_{2}(n). \tag{1.7}
\]

Step 5. Equivalence.  
Putting Step 3 and Step 4 together:

* If $T$ is acyclic, then $t=a$ and $U_{2}(T)=M_{2}(n)$.  
* If $T$ contains a directed $3$-cycle, it is non-transitive, hence $U_{2}(T)<M_{2}(n)$ by (1.6).

Therefore the equivalence $(\star)$ holds.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Part 2 - Uniqueness of $r=2$

(a) Trivial orders $r=0,1$.  
Because every player participates in $\binom{n-1}{0}=1$ and $\binom{n-1}{1}=n-1$ matches, respectively,

\[
U_{0}(T)=\sum_{i=1}^{n}(s_{i})_{0}=n,\qquad 
U_{1}(T)=\sum_{i=1}^{n}s_{i}=\binom{n}{2},
\]

both independent of the orientation of the edges.  Hence they cannot distinguish tournaments with or without $3$-cycles.

(b) Counter-examples for every $r\ge 3$.  

Fix $r\ge 3$ and set  

\[
n=r+2\quad(\text{so }n\ge r+1).\tag{2.1}
\]

Start with the \emph{transitive} tournament $T_{0}$ on the vertex set  
$P_{0}\succ P_{1}\succ\cdots\succ P_{n-1}$.  
Its (ascending) score sequence is  

\[
(0,1,2,\ldots ,n-1)=(0,1,2,\ldots ,r+1).\tag{2.2}
\]

Computation of $U_{r}(T_{0})$.  
For $k<r$ we have $(k)_{r}=0$; for $k=r$ and $k=r+1$ we obtain  

\[
(r)_{r}=r!,\qquad (r+1)_{r}=(r+1)!\!.
\]

Hence  

\[
U_{r}(T_{0})=(r)_{r}+(r+1)_{r}
=r!+(r+1)!=(r+2)\,r!
=\frac{(r+2)!}{\,r+1}=M_{r}(n). \tag{2.3}
\]

(Thus $T_{0}$ attains the upper bound.)

Creating a $3$-cycle without changing $U_{r}$.  
Reverse the three edges among $P_{0},P_{1},P_{2}$ so that  

\[
P_{0}\to P_{1},\quad P_{1}\to P_{2},\quad P_{2}\to P_{0}. \tag{2.4}
\]

All other edges remain as in $T_{0}$; call the new tournament $T$.

* Scores.  
Originally the three players had scores $0,1,2$.  After the modification they all have score $1$, hence still strictly less than $r$ (because $r\ge 3$).  Every other player keeps the same score.  Consequently

\[
(s_{0},s_{1},s_{2})\;:\;(0,1,2)\longrightarrow(1,1,1),
\]

so $(s_{i})_{r}$ remains $0$ for these three indices; the other $(s_{i})_{r}$'s are unchanged.  Therefore  

\[
U_{r}(T)=U_{r}(T_{0})=M_{r}(n). \tag{2.5}
\]

* Presence of a $3$-cycle.  
The vertices $P_{0},P_{1},P_{2}$ now form the directed cycle  
$P_{0}\to P_{1}\to P_{2}\to P_{0}$.

Thus, for every $r\ge 3$ we have produced $n\ge r+1$ and a tournament $T$ that contains a directed $3$-cycle but still satisfies $U_{r}(T)=M_{r}(n)$, contradicting $(\star)$.  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Conclusion.  

$U_{0}$ and $U_{1}$ are constant, while for every $r\ge 3$ the inequality $(\star)$ can fail even in the presence of a $3$-cycle.  Hence $r=2$ is the \emph{unique} index for which the functional $U_{r}$ detects $3$-cycles in all tournaments.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher-order statistic.  
Instead of the simple quadratic or cubic moment used in the original problems, the new criterion involves the r-th falling factorial (a degree-r polynomial) and works simultaneously for *all* r≥2.  Handling an arbitrary order requires deeper combinatorial identities (formula (2)) and understanding of polynomial convexity.

2. Use of majorisation and Schur-convexity.  
The solution demands knowledge of majorisation theory, Landau’s theorem on tournament score sequences, and Schur-convex functions—tools well beyond elementary inequalities.

3. Parameter-dependent proof.  
Because r is arbitrary, one cannot rely on ad-hoc edge-flipping calculations tailored to a specific power; instead a general structural argument (majorisation + convexity) is essential.

4. Multiple interacting concepts.  
The proof unites combinatorial enumeration (falling factorial sums), discrete convexity, inequality theory, and classical tournament structure theorems, requiring the solver to coordinate several advanced areas simultaneously.

Consequently the enhanced variant is substantially more technical and conceptually demanding than both the original problem and the current kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:

\[

Introduce the reference vector  

\[
a=(0,1,\ldots ,n-1).\tag{1.2}
\]

Because $\sum_{i\le m}a_{i}=m(m-1)/2$, inequality (1.1) rewrites as  

\[
\forall m:\;\sum_{i\le m}t_{i}\ge\sum_{i\le m}a_{i},
\qquad\text{with equality for }m=n. \tag{1.3}
\]

Thus, under the \emph{ascending} convention for majorisation,

\[
t\;\text{majorises}\;a,\qquad\text{briefly }t\succ a.\tag{1.4}
\]

Step 2. Schur-concavity of the quadratic falling factorial.  
Let $g(x)=x(x-1)$.  On $\mathbf Z$ we have  

\[
\Delta^{2}g(x)=g(x+2)-2g(x+1)+g(x)=2>0,
\]

so $g$ is convex.  A classical result (Hardy-Littlewood-Polya, inequality theory) says that \emph{for vectors sorted in the same order} a convex symmetric sum is \emph{Schur-concave} under the \emph{ascending} convention:  

\[
u\succ v,\;u\ne v\;\Longrightarrow\;\Phi(u)<\Phi(v), \tag{1.5}
\]

where  

\[
\Phi(z_{1},\ldots ,z_{n})=\sum_{i=1}^{n}g(z_{i})
=\sum_{i=1}^{n}(z_{i})_{2}=U_{2}(T).
\]

(The direction of the inequality is reversed compared with the more usual \emph{descending} convention; that is the only adaptation one must keep in mind.)

Step 3. Extremality of the transitive tournament.  
Apply (1.5) to $u=t$, $v=a$.  When $T$ is not transitive we have $t\ne a$ and thus  

\[
U_{2}(T)=\Phi(t)<\Phi(a). \tag{1.6}
\]

If $T$ \emph{is} transitive then $t=a$, hence $U_{2}(T)=\Phi(a)$.

Step 4. Evaluation of $\Phi(a)$.  
For the transitive scores $0,1,\ldots ,n-1$,

\[
\Phi(a)=\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}k(k-1)
=\frac{n(n-1)(n-2)}{3}=M_{2}(n). \tag{1.7}
\]

Step 5. Equivalence.  
Putting Step 3 and Step 4 together:

* If $T$ is acyclic, then $t=a$ and $U_{2}(T)=M_{2}(n)$.  
* If $T$ contains a directed $3$-cycle, it is non-transitive, hence $U_{2}(T)<M_{2}(n)$ by (1.6).

Therefore the equivalence $(\star)$ holds.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Part 2 - Uniqueness of $r=2$

(a) Trivial orders $r=0,1$.  
Because every player participates in $\binom{n-1}{0}=1$ and $\binom{n-1}{1}=n-1$ matches, respectively,

\[
U_{0}(T)=\sum_{i=1}^{n}(s_{i})_{0}=n,\qquad 
U_{1}(T)=\sum_{i=1}^{n}s_{i}=\binom{n}{2},
\]

both independent of the orientation of the edges.  Hence they cannot distinguish tournaments with or without $3$-cycles.

(b) Counter-examples for every $r\ge 3$.  

Fix $r\ge 3$ and set  

\[
n=r+2\quad(\text{so }n\ge r+1).\tag{2.1}
\]

Start with the \emph{transitive} tournament $T_{0}$ on the vertex set  
$P_{0}\succ P_{1}\succ\cdots\succ P_{n-1}$.  
Its (ascending) score sequence is  

\[
(0,1,2,\ldots ,n-1)=(0,1,2,\ldots ,r+1).\tag{2.2}
\]

Computation of $U_{r}(T_{0})$.  
For $k<r$ we have $(k)_{r}=0$; for $k=r$ and $k=r+1$ we obtain  

\[
(r)_{r}=r!,\qquad (r+1)_{r}=(r+1)!\!.
\]

Hence  

\[
U_{r}(T_{0})=(r)_{r}+(r+1)_{r}
=r!+(r+1)!=(r+2)\,r!
=\frac{(r+2)!}{\,r+1}=M_{r}(n). \tag{2.3}
\]

(Thus $T_{0}$ attains the upper bound.)

Creating a $3$-cycle without changing $U_{r}$.  
Reverse the three edges among $P_{0},P_{1},P_{2}$ so that  

\[
P_{0}\to P_{1},\quad P_{1}\to P_{2},\quad P_{2}\to P_{0}. \tag{2.4}
\]

All other edges remain as in $T_{0}$; call the new tournament $T$.

* Scores.  
Originally the three players had scores $0,1,2$.  After the modification they all have score $1$, hence still strictly less than $r$ (because $r\ge 3$).  Every other player keeps the same score.  Consequently

\[
(s_{0},s_{1},s_{2})\;:\;(0,1,2)\longrightarrow(1,1,1),
\]

so $(s_{i})_{r}$ remains $0$ for these three indices; the other $(s_{i})_{r}$'s are unchanged.  Therefore  

\[
U_{r}(T)=U_{r}(T_{0})=M_{r}(n). \tag{2.5}
\]

* Presence of a $3$-cycle.  
The vertices $P_{0},P_{1},P_{2}$ now form the directed cycle  
$P_{0}\to P_{1}\to P_{2}\to P_{0}$.

Thus, for every $r\ge 3$ we have produced $n\ge r+1$ and a tournament $T$ that contains a directed $3$-cycle but still satisfies $U_{r}(T)=M_{r}(n)$, contradicting $(\star)$.  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Conclusion.  

$U_{0}$ and $U_{1}$ are constant, while for every $r\ge 3$ the inequality $(\star)$ can fail even in the presence of a $3$-cycle.  Hence $r=2$ is the \emph{unique} index for which the functional $U_{r}$ detects $3$-cycles in all tournaments.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher-order statistic.  
Instead of the simple quadratic or cubic moment used in the original problems, the new criterion involves the r-th falling factorial (a degree-r polynomial) and works simultaneously for *all* r≥2.  Handling an arbitrary order requires deeper combinatorial identities (formula (2)) and understanding of polynomial convexity.

2. Use of majorisation and Schur-convexity.  
The solution demands knowledge of majorisation theory, Landau’s theorem on tournament score sequences, and Schur-convex functions—tools well beyond elementary inequalities.

3. Parameter-dependent proof.  
Because r is arbitrary, one cannot rely on ad-hoc edge-flipping calculations tailored to a specific power; instead a general structural argument (majorisation + convexity) is essential.

4. Multiple interacting concepts.  
The proof unites combinatorial enumeration (falling factorial sums), discrete convexity, inequality theory, and classical tournament structure theorems, requiring the solver to coordinate several advanced areas simultaneously.

Consequently the enhanced variant is substantially more technical and conceptually demanding than both the original problem and the current kernel variant.
